column5: The arrest of Rumeysa Ozturk, who is a valid visa holder, was seemingly linked to her pro-Palestinian activism. The use of immigration enforcement to penalize student activism under vague national security claims erodes democratic norms of free speech, setting a dangerous precedent for silencing critics through state power. The disregard of a federal court order requiring DHS to provide notice before transferring her to a Louisiana detention center reflects a dangerous erosion of judicial oversight and the executive branch bypassing checks and balances.
column6: Rumeysa Ozturk, a Turkish national and a student at Tufts with a valid F-1, was grabbed off the streets by plainclothes DHS officers wearing masks and using unmarked vehicles in Somerville, MA, on March 25, 2024. DHS alleges Ozturk engaged in activities supporting Hamas, leading to the termination of her visa. Additionally, a federal judge ordered DHS to provide 48 hours notice before moving her out of Massachusetts, but she was transferred to Louisiana without prior notice.

column3: U.S. deports hundreds of Venezuelans to El Salvador, despite court order
column4: Garrett, Luke. “U.S. Deports Hundreds of Venezuelans to El Salvador, despite Court Order.” NPR, NPR, 16 Mar. 2025, www.npr.org/2025/03/16/g-s1-54154/alien-enemies-el-salvador-trump.
column5: Trump administration deported hundreds of Venezuelans to El Salvador using the the Alien Enemies Act of 1798, which is used to to detain or deport nationals of an enemy nation during wartime or invasion, in violation of a court order.
column6: The Alien Enemies Act allows an expedited removal process, which means those subject to the president's declaration would not go through the normal immigration proceedings in court, or be able to claim asylum. Immigrant rights advocates fear that this will open the door for targeting and deporting others without court proceddings, regardless of their status or criminal records.

column3: U.S. Says Deportation of Maryland Man was an "Administrative Error
column4: Correal, Annie. "Maryland Man Mistakenly Deported to El Salvador, Now Held in Prison." The New York Times, 1 Apr. 2025, www.nytimes.com/2025/04/01/us/politics/maryland-man-deportation-error-el-salvador.html
column5: Kilmar Garcia had "withholding from removal" status in American courts, meaning he had a deportation order but was legally allowed to stay in the country due to the risk of harm in El Salvador. ICE agents disregarded this protection. Moreover, Garcia was not given a chance to contest his removal, violating his rights of due process under U.S. immigration law.
column6:
Kilmar Armando Abrego Garcia, a legally protected immigrant in the U.S. since 2019, was mistakenly deported to El Salvador due to an "administrative error" by the Trump administration. Despite his "withholding from removal" status, which legally allowed him to remain, immigration agents incorrectly told him his status had changed. He was deported on March 15 and is now imprisoned in El Salvador’s CECOT mega-prison.

The administration acknowledges the mistake but claims U.S. courts lack jurisdiction to order his return. His family has sued, seeking to pressure the U.S. government to intervene, but officials argue his case should not interfere with broader foreign policy objectives.

column3: Top I.R.S. officials are said to resign after a deal to give ICE migrants’ data.
column4: Kanno-Youngs, Zolan, and Alan Rappeport. "IRS to Share Immigrant Tax Records with ICE Under New Agreement." The New York Times, 8 Apr. 2025, www.nytimes.com/2025/04/08/us/politics/irs-ice-tax-data-deal.html.​:contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}
column5: Section 6103 of the Internal Revenue Code prohibits the release of tax information by an IRS employee without the taxpayer's consent. In the context of the article, the potential breach of taxpayer confidentiality would involve using this protected information for immigration enforcement against undocumented individuals, which has raised alarms among privacy advocates and legal experts.
column6: The IRS and ICE have created a controversial agreement which will ICE to use tax records to identify undocumented immigrants for deportation purposes. The deal, revealed in a court filing, allows ICE to request tax data about individuals who have been ordered to leave the U.S. or are under investigation. Advocacy groups and immigration lawyers have expressed alarm over the potential for increased deportations and the loss of trust among undocumented workers, many of whom fear that filing taxes could lead to their deportation.

column3: Trump Administration Directs Judges to Deny Asylum Without Hearings - NYT
column4: Jordan, Miriam. “Trump Administration Directs Judges to Deny Asylum Without Hearings.” The New York Times, 16 Apr. 2025. NYTimes.com, https://www.nytimes.com/2025/04/16/us/immigration-asylum-judges-policy.html.
column5: This new policy undermines legal norms that have allowed applicants a chance to clarify or present their claims in detail. Additionally, the new policy infringes on judicial independence by pressuring judges, who are expected to act impartially, to prioritize efficiency over individual legal assessment, thereby compromising their ability to exercise independent judgment.
column6: On April 11, 2025, a new policy from the Trump administration was implemented, instructing immigration judges to quickly reject asylum applications considered "legally deficient" without conducting full merit hearings. Normally, asylum seekers have the chance to thoroughly present their cases during merit hearings. However, this policy permits judges to decide cases without allowing asylum seekers to present evidence, supplement their claims, or testify, even when the facts of their cases are not in dispute.
column7: Critics argue that this guidance disregards due process and limits judicial independence.

Immigration judges are employees of the Department of Justice and fall under the President's authority.

column3: Former Tufts student detained over pro-Palestinian op-ed self-deports as part of Trump admin deal - Politico
column4: Gerstein, Josh, and Kyle Cheney. "Former Tufts student detained over pro-Palestinian op-ed self-deports as part of Trump admin deal." Politico, 17 April 2026. https://www.politico.com/news/2026/04/17/rumeysa-ozturk-deportation-case-00878694
column5: The pressure campaign to force a pro-Palestinian activist to leave the United States risks dissuading dissent among academics in the United States.  This hurts the ability for higher-education to freely disagree with the government.
column6: Rumyesa Ozturk, a Tufts graduate student, agreed to leave the United States as part of a settlement with the Trump administration. In 2025, Ozturk was detained by immigration authorities, who sought to deport her over her pro-Palestinian writing.

column3: Judges Fired After Blocking Deportations of Pro-Palestinian Students - NYT
column4: Aleaziz, Hamed, et. al. "Judges Fired After Blocking Deportations of Pro-Palestinian Students." The New York Times, 11 April 2026. https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/11/us/politics/immigration-judges-deportations-students.html
column5: Firing immigration judges who rule against the administration risks politicizing the civil service and encouraging loyalty to the President over the law. An apoliticial civil service was a longstanding democratic norm prior to the second Trump administration.
column6: The Trump administration fired immigration judges Roopal Patel and Nina Froes, who had dismissed deportation cases against pro-Palestinian international students. The judges oversaw cases against Rumeysa Ozturk and Mohsen Mahdawi.

column3: Memo by Rubio Approved Detention of Immigrant Who Criticized Trump - NYT
column4: Aleaziz, Hamed and Annie Correal. "Memo by Rubio Approved Detention of Immigrant Who Criticized Trump Ally." The New York Times, 19 June 2026. https://www.nytimes.com/2026/06/19/us/rubio-beto-coral-colombia.html?smid=url-share
column5: Seeking to deport an immigrant because of their political activism is an unprecedented move and risks stifling speech.
column6: Secretary of State Marco Rubio issued a memo determining that Franklin Humberto Coral Garrido was deportable from the United States. Mr. Rubio argued that Coral Garrido's presence in the United States undermines US Foreign Policy interests. Rubio also mentioned Coral Garrido's political activity in support of the Colombian President Gustavo Petro and his pending asylum application.

column3: Internal ICE records reveal widespread use of force in detention centers - WaPo
column4: MacMillan, Douglas, et. al. "Internal ICE records reveal widespread use of force in detention centers." The Washington Post, 4 May 2026. https://www.washingtonpost.com/business/2026/05/04/ice-detention-centers-force/
column5: The increase in force usage may indicate concerning conditions in ICE detention centers and the possible violation of detainees' constitutional rights. Without a full investigation, it is difficult to conclusively determine if constitutional rights have been violated, but the unprecedented increase in force is clear.
column6: During the first year of the Trump administration, ICE detention staff used force 37% more than in previous years, and the number of people subjected to force increased by 54%. At least 106 detainees have been injured in use-of-force incidents. In some cases, the incidents occurred after detainees asked for items they are legally entitled to, like food and water.
